Sourcing

Clothing Manufacturer vs Wholesale: Which Should Your Brand Use?

✎ FABRIOZA Team 📅 July 9, 2026 ☕ 10 min read
Home › Blog › Manufacturer vs Wholesale

Every clothing brand faces this fork in the road: buy wholesale garments and decorate them, or manufacture your own product from scratch. Choose wrong in either direction and you either burn months of margin on premium blanks, or sit on custom inventory you were not ready to sell. Here is the clear-eyed comparison, including the hybrid path most successful brands actually take.

Definitions First

  • Wholesale (blanks): you buy finished stock garments (Gildan, Bella+Canvas, AS Colour, Shaka) and add your graphics via a local printer or POD service. The garment itself is identical to what thousands of other brands use.
  • Custom manufacturing (cut & sew): a factory makes the garment from fabric rolls to your specification - your fit, fabric weight, colors, stitching, and labels. Nobody else can sell your exact product. That is what a cut & sew manufacturer does.

The Full Comparison

FactorWholesale blanksCustom manufacturing
Upfront cost$200–500 to start$800–2,000 for a first style run
Unit cost (quality tee)$8–15 (premium blank + print)$4.50–8.50 at 50–100 pcs factory-direct
Gross margin at $35 retail55–70%75–85%
Product uniquenessNone - same blank as everyoneTotal - your fit, fabric, details
BrandingRelabeling possible but adds cost/stepsWoven labels, tags, packaging built in
Speed to first saleDays4–6 weeks (samples + production)
Inventory riskMinimal (print on demand possible)Real but small at 50-piece MOQ
Scalability ceilingLow - blank supply and pricing you don't controlHigh - your product, any volume

What Wholesale Really Costs You

The blanks route looks cheaper until you do margin math at scale. A premium heavyweight blank runs $9–12; add $2–4 printing and you are at $11–16 per unit — often more than a fully custom equivalent from a factory. And you still cannot control the three things customers use to judge quality: the fit, the fabric hand-feel, and the neck label. There is a reason every brand you admire moved off blanks: you cannot build a defensible brand on a garment anyone can buy. The moment a design works, a competitor can copy it on the identical blank within a week.

What Custom Manufacturing Really Costs You

Honesty in the other direction: custom means committing before you have sales. You wait 4–6 weeks for samples and production (full t-shirt cost breakdown here), you pay for a size run per style, and your first order requires decisions — GSM, fit, trims — that blanks made for you. The traditional version of this risk was brutal: 300–500 piece MOQs meant $5,000+ committed per style. The modern version is far gentler: at a 50-piece MOQ, a custom style costs $300–900 to produce — comparable to a serious blanks order, with triple the margin headroom and a product that is actually yours.

The Hybrid Strategy Smart Brands Use

  1. Phase 1 — Validate with blanks (month 0–2): test 3–5 designs on quality blanks or POD. You are testing graphics and audience, not garments. Spend as little as possible.
  2. Phase 2 — Convert winners to custom (month 2–4): take the 1–2 designs that sold and produce them properly: heavyweight fabric, your fit, woven labels, 50 pieces each. Announce it as an upgrade — "now on our own 240 GSM heavyweight blank" is a genuinely compelling relaunch story, and your margin jumps 15–20 points on day one.
  3. Phase 3 — All-custom with a reorder engine (month 4+): new designs launch custom by default; winners get reordered in 2–4 week cycles. Blanks retire to sample/testing duty only.

This sequence spends validation money on blanks where it is cheap, and brand money on manufacturing where it compounds. The common failure mode is staying in Phase 1 for two years because it feels safe — while margin and differentiation stay capped forever.

Decision Checklist

Stay wholesale (for now) if: you have not sold anything yet, your designs are unvalidated, you have under $800 total, or your model is genuinely print-on-demand (one-off designs, zero inventory).

Go custom manufacturing if: you have a design that sells (even 20–30 units), customers comment on fit or fabric, your margin cannot fund marketing, you are relabeling blanks anyway, or competitors run the same blank. Two or more of those = you are past due. Start by reading our manufacturer vetting guide, then get quotes on your best seller - our pricing page and MOQ calculator give you the numbers in minutes, and our private label service handles labels, tags and packaging in one order.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is it cheaper to buy wholesale or manufacture clothing?

Per unit, custom manufacturing is usually cheaper than premium blanks once you order 50+ pieces: a fully custom heavyweight tee runs $4.50–8.50 factory-direct versus $11–16 for a premium blank plus printing. Wholesale is only cheaper at very small quantities or with budget-tier blanks.

Can I start a clothing brand with wholesale blanks?

Yes, and it is a sensible way to validate designs cheaply. The limitation is differentiation: the garment is identical to thousands of other brands, margins cap around 55–70%, and you control neither fit nor fabric. Most brands switch to custom manufacturing once a design proves itself.

What MOQ do I need for custom clothing manufacturing?

Traditional factories require 300–500 pieces per style. Small-batch factories like FABRIOZA produce full custom garments from 50 pieces per style, which brings a first custom run to $300–900 - comparable to a premium blanks order.

How long does custom manufacturing take vs wholesale?

Wholesale blanks can be printed and selling within days. Custom manufacturing takes 5–7 days for samples plus 2–4 weeks for bulk production and 3–7 days shipping - roughly 4–6 weeks from design to sellable inventory.

What is private label clothing?

Private label sits between the two: a manufacturer produces garments (from their developed patterns or your spec) branded entirely as yours - woven labels, hang tags, packaging. It is the fastest route to a fully branded product without developing every pattern from scratch.

Ready to Move From Blanks to Your Own Product?

FABRIOZA makes the jump easy: 50-piece MOQ, your fit and fabric, custom labels included - at prices that compete with premium wholesale blanks.
